main_paper.raw: {"methods":["CoDiCast: a conditional diffusion model for global weather prediction","Use of cross-attention mechanism to integrate past observations into the denoising process","Uncertainty quantification via repeated sampling from stochastic Gaussian noise and multiple denoising runs"],"problems":["Accurate global weather prediction","Uncertainty quantification in weather forecasting","Reducing computational cost of ensemble weather prediction"],"datasets":["ERA5 reanalysis data from the European Centre for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts (ECMWF)"],"metrics":["Accuracy","Inference speed (computational efficiency)","Uncertainty quantification"],"results":[{"metric":"Forecast time for 3-day global weather (6-hour steps, 5.625° resolution, 5+ variables)","value":"~12 minutes on a single A100 GPU (80GB)"},{"metric":"Accuracy","value":"Outperforms several existing MLWP methods (exact numbers not specified in provided text)"},{"metric":"Inference speed","value":"Faster than NWP models"}],"novelty_claims":["First to apply conditional diffusion models for global weather prediction with uncertainty quantification.","CoDiCast achieves a trade-off between accuracy, uncertainty quantification, and computational efficiency.","Integration of cross-attention mechanism to condition on past observations in the diffusion process.","Demonstrates that diffusion models can generate diverse, realistic weather scenarios, capturing uncertainty."]}

methods: CoDiCast: a conditional diffusion model for global weather prediction
Use of cross-attention mechanism to integrate past observations into the denoising process
Uncertainty quantification via repeated sampling from stochastic Gaussian noise and multiple denoising runs
problems: Accurate global weather prediction
Uncertainty quantification in weather forecasting
Reducing computational cost of ensemble weather prediction
datasets: ERA5 reanalysis data from the European Centre for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts (ECMWF)

results.metric: Forecast time for 3-day global weather (6-hour steps, 5.625° resolution, 5+ variables)
results.value: ~12 minutes on a single A100 GPU (80GB)
Outperforms several existing MLWP methods (exact numbers not specified in provided text)
Faster than NWP models

novelty_claims: First to apply conditional diffusion models for global weather prediction with uncertainty quantification.
CoDiCast achieves a trade-off between accuracy, uncertainty quantification, and computational efficiency.
Integration of cross-attention mechanism to condition on past observations in the diffusion process.
Demonstrates that diffusion models can generate diverse, realistic weather scenarios, capturing uncertainty.

methods: Multi-modal and multi-task deep learning architecture for weather forecasting
Model-specific encoder-decoders for each atmospheric variable (modality)
Cross-modal fusion Transformer to model interactions among variables

11.5 days

novelty_claims: First AI-based system to extend skillful global medium-range weather forecast lead time to 10.75 days (z500, ACC > 0.6)
Treats each atmospheric variable as a separate modality and task, enabling multi-modal, multi-task learning for weather forecasting
Introduces a cross-modal fusion Transformer for modeling interactions among atmospheric variables
Applies uncertainty loss for region-adaptive, multi-task optimization in weather forecasting
Proposes a replay buffer mechanism to efficiently improve long-lead forecast performance in AI-driven weather prediction

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Cascade machine learning model architecture (FuXi) with separate models optimized for 0-5, 5-10, and 10-15 day forecast windows","Autoregressive modeling for sequential weather prediction","Ensemble forecasting by perturbing initial conditions and model parameters"],"problems":["Accumulation of forecast errors in long-term (15-day) machine learning-based weather forecasts","Inability of single ML models to achieve optimal performance across both short and long lead times","Need for ML-based weather forecasts to match or surpass the performance of ECMWF ensemble mean (EM) and extend skillful forecast lead time beyond 10 days"],"datasets":["ECMWF ERA5 reanalysis dataset (39 years, 6-hourly, 0.25° spatial resolution)"],"metrics":["Anomaly Correlation Coefficient (ACC)","Continuous Ranked Probability Score (CRPS)"],"results":[{"metric":"Skillful forecast lead time (ACC > 0.6) for Z500","value":"10.5 days (FuXi) vs 9.25 days (ECMWF HRES)"},{"metric":"Skillful forecast lead time (ACC > 0.6) for T2M","value":"14.5 days (FuXi) vs 10 days (ECMWF HRES)"},{"metric":"CRPS (ensemble performance) for Z500, T850, MSL, T2M","value":"FuXi ensemble comparable to ECMWF ensemble within 9-day lead time"}],"novelty_claims":["First ML-based system to achieve comparable performance to ECMWF ensemble mean (EM) in 15-day global weather forecasts at 0.25° resolution","Introduction of a cascade ML model architecture with models optimized for different forecast time windows to reduce accumulation errors and improve long-term forecast accuracy","FuXi extends the skillful forecast lead time (ACC > 0.6) for Z500 and T2M beyond that of ECMWF HRES","FuXi ensemble provides reliable uncertainty estimates and skillful ensemble forecasts comparable to ECMWF ensemble for medium-range forecasts"]}

methods: Generative Ensemble Diffusion (GED): a diffusion model that generates multiple possible weather scenarios and combines them via a post-processing network

problems: Precipitation nowcasting (short-term weather forecasting, typically 2-6 hours ahead)

novelty_claims: Proposes the Generative Ensemble Diffusion (GED) approach for precipitation nowcasting, which uses a diffusion model to generate multiple scenarios and combines them via a post-processing network.

methods: FengWu-GHR: a data-driven global weather forecasting model at 0.09° (about 9km) horizontal resolution
Knowledge inheritance from a pretrained low-resolution model to enable high-resolution forecasting
General framework for downscaling ML-based weather forecast models from low to high resolution
problems: Enabling kilometer-scale (high-resolution) global weather forecasting using data-driven (ML-based) models
Overcoming the lack of large-scale, high-resolution global weather datasets for training ML models
Reducing the computational cost of training high-resolution ML-based weather forecast models
Improving the accuracy and detail of medium-range weather forecasts, especially for extreme events and station-level predictions
datasets: ECMWF high-resolution products (from 2016 onwards)
Station observations (for evaluation)

results.value: >4 billion
Superior to IFS-HRES in 2022 hindcast
Smallest error among compared models
Earlier and more accurate detection of extreme events

novelty_claims: FengWu-GHR is the first data-driven global weather forecasting model operating at 0.09° (kilometer-scale) horizontal resolution, matching operational IFS-HRES.
It is the most parameter-rich forecasting model in atmospheric science (>4 billion parameters).
It introduces a general and transferable framework for upgrading ML-based weather forecast models from low to high resolution with minimal cost.
FengWu-GHR achieves better consistency with real-time analysis data and outperforms both physical and data-driven models in fine-grained global forecasts.
It demonstrates improved accuracy for station-level predictions and better stability for long lead-time forecasts, including reliable extreme event forecasting.

methods: Initial condition (IC)-based ensemble forecasting with Pangu-Weather (using random noise, perturbed initial conditions from NWP, and perturbations from past data)

results.metric: Forecast skill (Pangu-Weather with UQ methods vs. ECMWF ensemble)
results.value: Pangu-Weather with UQ methods provides improvements over ECMWF ensemble forecasts for lead times up to 5 days (quantitative values not specified in the provided text)

novelty_claims: Systematic study and comparison of uncertainty quantification methods for generating probabilistic forecasts from a state-of-the-art deterministic data-driven weather model (Pangu-Weather)
Application and evaluation of both initial condition-based ensemble and post-hoc statistical/machine learning UQ methods to data-driven weather models
Demonstration that probabilistic forecasts from data-driven models can outperform physics-based ensemble forecasts for medium-range weather prediction

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["GraphCast: a machine learning-based method for global medium-range weather forecasting trained on reanalysis data"],"problems":["Improving the accuracy and efficiency of global medium-range weather forecasting","Enabling direct use of historical weather data to improve forecasting models","Enhancing prediction of severe weather events such as tropical cyclones, atmospheric rivers, and extreme temperatures"],"datasets":["Reanalysis data (specific dataset not named, but refers to global reanalysis datasets)"],"metrics":["Forecast accuracy (compared to operational deterministic systems)","Percentage of verification targets outperformed"],"results":[{"metric":"Percentage of verification targets outperformed","value":"90% of 1380 verification targets"},{"metric":"Forecast time","value":"under one minute for 10-day global forecast at 0.25 degree resolution"}],"novelty_claims":["GraphCast is a machine learning-based forecasting system trained directly from reanalysis data, unlike traditional numerical weather prediction models.","GraphCast predicts hundreds of weather variables globally at high resolution (0.25 degree) for up to 10 days in under one minute.","GraphCast significantly outperforms the most accurate operational deterministic systems on the majority of verification targets.","GraphCast enables better prediction of severe weather events, demonstrating the potential of machine learning for complex dynamical systems."]}

problems: Improving the accuracy and efficiency of global medium-range weather forecasting

novelty_claims: GraphCast is a machine learning-based forecasting system trained directly from reanalysis data, unlike traditional numerical weather prediction models.
GraphCast predicts hundreds of weather variables globally at high resolution (0.25 degree) for up to 10 days in under one minute.
GraphCast significantly outperforms the most accurate operational deterministic systems on the majority of verification targets.
GraphCast enables better prediction of severe weather events, demonstrating the potential of machine learning for complex dynamical systems.

novelty_claims: First deep learning model to forecast surface winds on global scales with high accuracy and resolution.
First DL model to resolve small-scale structures in global precipitation forecasts at 0.25° resolution.
Eight times higher resolution than previous DL-based global weather models, enabling accurate representation of extreme events (e.g., tropical cyclones, atmospheric rivers).
Orders of magnitude faster than traditional NWP models, enabling rapid, large-ensemble probabilistic forecasting (e.g., 1,000-member ensembles in seconds).
Combines Fourier-based token mixing with a vision transformer backbone for state-of-the-art high-resolution, scalable, and accurate weather forecasting.

methods: Stormer: a simple transformer-based model for weather forecasting based on a standard vision transformer (ViT) backbone
Weather-specific embedding layer to transform input data into tokens modeling atmospheric variable interactions
Randomized dynamics forecasting objective: trains the model to predict weather at random intervals, enabling multiple forecasts for a target lead time
Pressure-weighted loss: weights variables at different pressure levels to approximate density at each level
Combining multiple forecasts at inference for improved accuracy, especially at long lead times
Extensive ablation studies to identify key components
problems: Improving the skill and reliability of medium-range weather forecasting
Reducing the complexity and compute requirements of deep learning-based weather forecasting models
Identifying which architectural components are critical for success in data-driven weather forecasting
Establishing a common, simplified framework for data-driven weather forecasting models

Training data and compute efficiency (e.g., GPU hours, data resolution)

Outperforms current state-of-the-art methods
More than 5× lower than baselines
Orders-of-magnitude fewer than baselines

novelty_claims: Introduction of Stormer, a simple transformer model achieving state-of-the-art performance with minimal changes to the standard transformer backbone

Randomized dynamics forecasting objective enabling multiple forecasts for a target lead time and improved accuracy, especially for long lead times
Demonstration that state-of-the-art performance can be achieved with much less data and compute than previous methods
Favorable scaling properties: consistent improvements in accuracy with increased model size and training tokens

methods: W-MAE: Weather model with Masked AutoEncoder pre-training for weather forecasting
Self-supervised pre-training to reconstruct spatial correlations within meteorological variables
Fine-tuning pre-trained W-MAE for future state prediction of meteorological variables (temporal modeling)
Use of Vision Transformer (ViT) as backbone network
Modified MAE decoder with self-attention applied only to the second dimension to reduce computational overhead
Task-agnostic pre-training schema that can be integrated with task-specific models (e.g., FourCastNet)
problems: Accurate multi-variable weather forecasting with strong spatiotemporal dependencies
Effective utilization of massive, mostly unlabeled meteorological data
Alleviating cumulative error in multi-step weather prediction
Improving performance on diagnostic variable forecasting (e.g., precipitation)

Handling heterogeneous and noisy meteorological data integration

metrics: Performance on 10-day prediction (likely ACC or RMSE, but only ACC is explicitly mentioned)

results.value: W-MAE yields an average 20% improvement in ACC when applied to FourCastNet for precipitation forecasting
W-MAE achieves stable 10-day prediction performance for meteorological variables (e.g., low-level winds, geopotential height at 500 hPa) without explicit error accumulation optimization
Fine-tuning a pre-trained FourCastNet (with W-MAE) is nearly 1.2x faster than direct training

novelty_claims: First large-scale weather model explicitly designed to consider spatial dependencies within meteorological data using MAE and ViT
Introduction of self-supervised pre-training (MAE) to weather forecasting, enabling effective use of large-scale unlabeled meteorological data
Task-agnostic pre-training schema that can be easily integrated with various task-specific models, providing generalizable representations for downstream tasks
Demonstrated significant reduction in cumulative error and improved robustness in multi-step and diagnostic variable forecasting
Demonstrated high efficiency and performance in large-member ensemble forecasts (e.g., 100-member ensemble)
